title: Cynanchum oxyphyllum
taxonomy: Cynanchum oxyphyllum is a species within the genus Cynanchum, which is a group of plants in the family Apocynaceae. The Apocynaceae family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants, commonly known as dogbane or oleander family. This family is part of the order Gentianales, a group of flowering plants that also includes the families Loganiaceae and Gelsemiaceae. The Apocynaceae family is further divided into various subfamilies, with Cynanchum being part of the subfamily Asclepiadoideae. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, such as its leaf and flower morphology, which are typical of the Apocynaceae family.
uses: Cynanchum oxyphyllum is used in traditional medicine, primarily in Asia, for its purported health benefits. The plant is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help alleviate conditions such as fever and digestive issues. In some cultures, it is used to treat respiratory problems and skin conditions. The plant's roots are said to have a sedative effect, which may aid in promoting sleep and reducing anxiety. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy of these medicinal uses.

side_effects: Cynanchum oxyphyllum, like many other plants in the Asclepiadaceae family, may cause gastrointestinal issues and allergic reactions in some individuals if ingested or handled improperly. Its sap may also cause skin irritation and dermatitis in sensitive people. In rare cases, ingesting Cynanchum oxyphyllum may lead to more severe health problems, such as cardiac arrest. intro: Cynanchum oxyphyllum, also known as Oxyphyllum, is a plant species in the Apocynaceae family, traditionally used in herbal medicine for its therapeutic properties. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects, which may aid in the treatment of various ailments such as fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Cynanchum oxyphyllum include alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Cynanchum oxyphyllum is often prepared as a decoction, tea, or tincture, and is used to treat various health conditions, including fever, cough, and respiratory infections.
